Subject: The Ford Factor keeps on factoring
Body: This Week in Politics To take on the province, it matters how Toronto’s next mayor wins ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ THIS WEEK IN POLITICS By Edward Keenan Illustration by Nathan Pilla/Toronto Star This week, even as the candidates for mayor continued to jockey over who might be best equipped to deal with the provincial government and Bully Premier Doug Ford, other GTA mayors were getting examples of the challenges. Markham Mayor Frank Scarpitti floated the idea of amalgamating the cities of York, as was once done to the cities that now make up Toronto, only to have Ford shoot him down within 24 hours. (“We aren’t doing it,” he said plainly.) And Mississauga Mayor Bonnie Crombie, with the wind in her sails after Ford granted her wish to de-amalgamate Mississauga from its own regional government, was finding herself Ford’s target as she launched a bid to oppose him from the Liberal leader’s bench at Queen’s Park. Meanwhile, even as I continued to think about how a mayor can deal with the Ford Factor — a question I recently put to Jennifer Keesmaat in a podcast after writing about it myself — I got some thoughts from a long-time watcher of politics around these parts who did not want to be named or quoted. This analyst’s current theory of Toronto-provincial relations holds that because people in this province have a relatively weak provincial identity (unlike Quebec, Newfoundland or Alberta, that is, they think of themselves as more residents of their cities than of Ontario), a mayor of Toronto can roll over the provincial government if they are proactive and present a clear — and popular — argument. Witness how Mel Lastman got the province to cough up money after amalgamation, how David Miller got the province to give gas tax dollars for transit and, I would add, how Rob Ford got the province to join him in Scarborough subway follies. Don't miss a thing from your favourite Star columnists. Always get the latest from the voices that matter most to you: pick the columnists you'd like to follow here, and you’ll get an email every time they have a new article online. The province holds statutory authority, of course, and will sometimes use it. But the city can win public relations battles, and in politics that always matters — and often triumphs. This is something my colleague Andrew Phillips wrote about in his own way this week — arguing that for all the “strong mayor” fights we’ve been having about formal authority this year (and they’ll soon be having in 26 other cities, it was announced late in the week), what Toronto needs now is a strong mayor in the old-fashioned sense, one with the moral authority to stand up for the city. He worries that if any of these mayors is elected with a slim minority of voter support — as appears likely, according to most polls with Olivia Chow’s commanding lead consisting of a third or less of total voters — that will weaken them as they go into battle with Ford. It’s possible. But how that victory is accomplished might matter too. If Chow were to win doubling the next closest contender, as it appears from polling averages she could, or if other ideologically similar candidates such as Josh Matlow and Mitzie Hunter place strongly in the slots behind her in the top four or five, that could strengthen the message from voters that Torontonians are thinking a certain way. And, as Phillips concludes, “There’s something else that would strengthen the mayor’s hand: a high turnout.” Voter engagement has plummeted since John Tory first beat Ford and Chow in the 2014 election, from 60 per cent then to a desperately sad 30 per cent in the election last year. Early voting, which closed this week, offers some hope that people are a bit more active this time around — it was up 12 per cent over last fall’s election. It’s a start. Will the uncertainty and range of choices in this election get more people out to vote? It’s unclear. And which of these candidates is best equipped to deal with Ford? That’s unclear too. But it seems that the answer to the former could affect how any new mayor is able to answer the latter. As in so many things, it’s a question that’s up to the voters. Here’s more of the Star’s Toronto election coverage from my colleagues: Who should you vote for? Take the Vote Compass survey to see which candidate is right for you. Olivia Chow still has a sizeable lead. How far behind is everyone else? Here’s what the polls show. Ana Bailão is a consensus builder. But can she be the fighter Toronto needs? Brad Bradford is an agent of change. Some worry he’s changed too much. Olivia Chow is the great organizer of the left. Can she be a unifying mayor? Anthony Furey is a media pundit with deeply right-wing views. Why doesn’t he want to talk about that now? Edward Keenan is a Star city columnist. Follow him on Twitter: @TheKeenanWire. If you do not confirm your address until 07 september 2006 your account will be SUSPENDED for security reasons and we will send you an Activation Code by post which you will need to renew your online banking service access. You will receive this within seven days if your current address is not confirmed. * Please do not respond to this email as your reply will not be received. Thanks for your patience as we work together to protect your account. Yours sincerely Adrian Grace Security Department Barclays International Insurance Services Company Limited

Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots
Body: Excellent, many thanks, that works a treat! But I don't understand how... >bquote(.(tag) ~ Delta * .(suffix)) "A)" ~ Delta * "stbA::cat" Yet when I use: plot(1, main = bquote(.(tag) ~ Delta * .(suffix))) It works fine in the plot. Must be magic ;) -----Original Message----- From: Gabor Grothendieck [mailto:ggrothendieck@gmail.com] Sent: Tue 05/08/2008 6:18 PM To: michael watson (IAH-C) Cc: r-help@r-project.org Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots Try bquote / substitute as has been mentioned: tag <- "A)" suffix <- "stbA::cat" plot(1, main = bquote(.(tag) ~ Delta * .(suffix)))) 2008/8/5 michael watson (IAH-C) : > OK, I am definitely struggling here. > > The text I am trying to plot isn't a mathematical expression, it's: > > A) ?stbA::cat > > This is biological notation for a particular mutation. The "A)" part is a tag identifying the graph, and the "stbA::cat" bit is the gene that is mutated. Both the tag and gene are set as variables in a loop, and only the delta remains the same. > > As the text I am trying to plot isn't a mathematical expression, I seem to be having trouble getting substitute() and expression() to do what I want. As plotmath states "A mathematical expression must obey the normal rules of syntax for any R expression", I fear I may be barking up the wrong tree! > > Thank you all for your help so far, but can someone please help me once more and tell me how I can get the capital Delta symbol so I can use it in non-mathematical text and strings for plotting? > > Thanks once again > > Mick > > -----Original Message----- > From: Prof Brian Ripley [mailto:ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk] > Sent: 05 August 2008 12:40 > To: michael watson (IAH-C) > Cc: Henrique Dallazuanna; r-help@r-project.org > Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots > > On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, michael watson (IAH-C) wrote: > >> Excellent! I even tried DELTA but not Delta.... > > ?plotmath says > > 'Alpha' - 'Omega' uppercase Greek symbols > > How could that be made clearer? > > (Note it carefully says Greek *symbols* not *character*: they are not the > same thing.) > >> Now, how do I go about pasting that with some text that I have in various other variables? > > Use substitute? E.g. > > substitute(expression(foo*Delta), list(foo=3)) > > (Some people prefer bquote, but that is just a wrapper for substitute in > R.) > >> >> -----Original Message----- >> From: Henrique Dallazuanna [mailto:wwwhsd@gmail.com] >> Sent: 05 August 2008 12:17 >> To: michael watson (IAH-C) >> Cc: r-help@r-project.org >> Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots >> >> Try, >> >> expression(Delta) >> >> On Tue, Aug 5, 2008 at 8:14 AM, michael watson (IAH-C) >> wrote: >>> Hi >>> >>> I am running an R script that creates 100s of graphs, and I need to use >>> the greek CAPITAL letter delta in the mtext() function. >>> >>> I got as far as expression(delta) but this gives me the lowercase >>> version. >>> >>> Can anyone help? I'm using R 2.7 on Windows XP >>> >>> Mick >>> >>> Head of Informatics >>> Institute for Animal Health >>> Compton >>> Berks >>> RG20 7NN >>> 01635 578411 >>> >>> http://www.iah.ac.uk/research/bioinformatics/bioinf.shtml >>> >>> The information contained in this message may be confide...{{dropped:14}} >>> >>> ______________________________________________ >>> R-help@r-project.org mailing list >>> https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-help >>> PLEASE do read the posting guide http://www.R-project.org/posting-guide.html >>> and provide commented, minimal, self-contained, reproducible code. >>> >> >> >> >> -- >> Henrique Dallazuanna >> Curitiba-ParanÃ¡-Brasil >> 25Â° 25' 40" S 49Â° 16' 22" O >> >> ______________________________________________ >> R-help@r-project.org mailing list >> https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-help >> PLEASE do read the posting guide http://www.R-project.org/posting-guide.html >> and provide commented, minimal, self-contained, reproducible code. >> > > -- > Brian D. Subject: Re: Urgent: Discrepancy in API Response Time on Dashboard
Body:
Dear Isiah,

Thank you for contacting Aetheros Support. We understand that you are experiencing a discrepancy in your API response time on the dashboard compared to your internal logs.

To assist us in investigating this further, could you please provide us with the following information:

* **Aetheros Account ID:**
* **Name of the affected API:**
* **Timeframe of the discrepancy:**
* **Screenshots of the dashboard showing the issue:**
* **A brief explanation of your Mercury Language instrumentation for this particular endpoint:**

Once we have this information, we will be able to investigate the issue and provide you with a solution as soon as possible.

Thank you for your patience and cooperation.

Sincerely,
Aetheros Support
